name: Coins
description: A spendable currency balance used as the default unit for rewards, purchases, fees, and sinks.
design_notes: Coins work best when every source and sink has a named purpose. A coin economy needs separate tracking for reward grants, purchase debits, sellback credits, refunds, and compensation so designers can detect inflation instead of only seeing the current wallet total.
design_purpose: Give players an easy-to-understand measure of buying power.
Connect rewards, shops, repairs, upgrades, and resource conversion through one shared unit.
Create tunable sinks that keep repeated reward drops meaningful over long play sessions.
player_fantasy: The player feels that every run, fight, or sale adds visible purchasing power toward the next useful choice.

parameters.name: currency_id
parameters.description: Canonical identifier for the balance so wallets, stores, drops, and saves reference the same currency.

parameters.name: wallet_capacity
parameters.description: Maximum stored balance before overflow handling, banking, or reward rejection applies.

parameters.name: currency_source_id
parameters.description: Identifier written to grant logs so the team can audit where currency enters the economy.

parameters.name: currency_sink_id
parameters.description: Identifier written to spend logs so sinks can be tuned against sources.

parameters.name: minor_unit_scale
parameters.description: Storage multiplier used to avoid floating point currency errors when fractional prices exist.

edge_cases: A reward grant would exceed wallet_capacity and needs a cap, overflow bank, or explicit rejection message.
A purchase and reward arrive in the same frame or server tick and must resolve in a deterministic transaction order.
Refunds after a failed purchase must restore the exact debit amount and currency_id, not a recalculated current price.
Offline or cross-device saves can replay an already-claimed reward unless grant IDs are idempotent.
common_bugs: Wallet UI increments optimistically but the server rejects the transaction, leaving the client balance wrong until reload.
Coin rewards use floats for display and storage, creating rounding drift after many small grants.
Sellback, repair, and upgrade systems each use different currency IDs for what players see as the same coin balance.
Duplicate reward messages apply the same transaction_id twice because grant logs are not checked before crediting.
balancing_notes: Track source and sink totals by currency_source_id and currency_sink_id; inflation is easier to fix when the dominant faucet is visible.
Early prices should teach the value of coins, while late sinks need enough capacity to absorb farming without making casual play feel stalled.
If coins drop from every action, add meaningful sinks or conversion routes before the wallet becomes decorative.
accessibility_notes: Show wallet changes as readable deltas with icons and text, not only tiny animated numbers.
Use grouping separators or compact notation options for large balances so players can distinguish 10000 from 100000 quickly.

implementation_notes.unity: Store currency_id balances in a wallet service, not per-shop MonoBehaviours. Apply rewards and purchases through transaction_id records, then update UI Toolkit or UGUI from committed wallet events.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep the wallet in an Autoload or saved Resource with grant_currency and spend_currency methods that require currency_source_id or currency_sink_id and reject duplicate transaction_id values.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use a replicated PlayerState or inventory component for wallet balances; server-authoritative purchase RPCs should debit currency, grant items, and persist the transaction atomically.
implementation_notes.web: Represent balances as integers in minor units, commit wallet mutations through a transaction reducer, and persist grant logs with the save so browser refreshes cannot duplicate rewards.

relationships.reason: Coins provide the wallet balance spent by shop purchase transactions.
Coin rewards can fund long-term upgrade purchases and progression sinks.
Stable currency units make price modifiers and sellback ratios easier to audit and display.

example_games.title: Super Mario Bros.
example_games.note: Uses coins as a visible collectible reward.

example_games.title: Stardew Valley
example_games.note: Uses money as the main farming and upgrade currency.
